For Investor A shares: Total 1.13%; Net, Including Investment Related Expenses 1.05%. Institutional and Investor A shares have contractual waivers with an end date of 06/30/2026 terminable upon 90 days' notice. For certain share classes, BlackRock may voluntarily agree to waive certain fees and expenses in which the adviser may discontinue at any time without notice. Expenses stated as of the fund’s most recent prospectus. Data represents past performance and is no guarantee of future results. Investment returns and principal values may fluctuate so that an investor s shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than their original cost. All returns assume reinvestment of dividends and capital gains. Current performance may be lower or higher than that shown. Refer to blackrock.com for most recent month-end performance. Investment returns reflect total fund operating expenses, net of all fees, waivers and/or expense reimbursements. Index performance is shown for illustrative purposes only. It is not possible to invest directly in an unmanaged index. Share classes have different sales charges, fees and other features. Returns with sales charge reflect deduction of current maximum initial sales charge of 5.25% for Investor A shares. Institutional shares have no front- or back-end load. Institutional shares have limited availability and may be purchased at various minimums. See prospectus for details. Net Expenses Excluding Investment Related Expenses for Institutional shares: 0.80%; for Investor A shares: 1.05%.

Commentary as of 03/31/25

  • The fund posted returns of -13.35% (Institutional shares) and -13.43% (Investor A shares, without sales charge) for the first quarter of 2025.
  • The largest contributors to relative performance were security selection in the financials sector, positioning in the industrials sector, and investment decisions in the consumer discretionary sector. The largest detractor from relative returns was stock selection in the information technology (IT) and communication services sectors, as well as positioning in the utilities sector.
  • The largest exposures were in the IT, industrials, and health care sectors. During the quarter, the fund increased its allocations to the financials and IT sectors, and reduced its exposures to the health care and materials sectors.

Contributors

Detractors
Stock selection in the real estate sector was the largest contributor to relative performance. Notably, security selection in the real estate management & development industry proved beneficial. Investment decisions in the consumer staples sector, namely in the textiles, apparel & luxury goods industry, boosted relative results. Lastly, in the consumer staples sector, an avoidance of the personal care products industry added value.

The largest detractor was stock selection in the IT sector. Notably, stock selection in the semiconductors & semiconductor equipment industry dampened relative results. In the health care sector, underweight positioning in the health care providers & services industry hindered performance. Another meaningful detractor included security selection in the industrials sector, particularly an overweight allocation to the electrical equipment industry.

Further insight

During the quarter, U.S. equities faced elevated uncertainty, with sharp daily trading moves and heavy macroeconomic influence, akin to emerging markets. Investors had to navigate a rapidly shifting landscape due to geopolitical developments and changing growth and interest rate expectations. Fluid tariff implications add headline risk and supply-chain pressures, which are impacting corporate margins and global trade. Despite this, the U.S. economy starts from a solid footing, with strong corporate and household balance sheets, high profit margins, and a healthy labor market. Potential policy tailwinds, such as deregulation and tax cuts, could boost business confidence and investment activity.

Investment approach

Invests at least 80% of its net assets in equity securities issued by U.S. mid- capitalization growth companies that fund management team believes have above-average earnings growth potential. Although a universal definition of mid-capitalization companies does not exist, the fund generally defines these companies as those with market capitalizations comparable in size to companies in the Russell Midcap Growth Index.

Important Risks: The fund is actively managed and its characteristics will vary. Holdings shown should not be deemed as a recommendation to buy or sell securities. Stock and bond values fluctuate in price so the value of your investment can go down depending on market condi- tions. Investing in mid-cap companies may entail greater risk than large-cap companies, due to shorter operating histories, less seasoned management or lower trading volumes. The fund may use derivatives to hedge its investments or to seek to enhance returns. Derivatives entail risks relating to liquidity, leverage and credit that may reduce returns and increase volatility. Performance may be attributable to unusually high IPO profits. There is no guarantee this level of performance will be repeated. IPO securities have no trading history and the price may be volatile.

The opinions expressed are those of the fund’s portfolio management team as of March 31, 2025, and may change as subsequent conditions vary. Information and opinions are derived from proprietary and nonproprietary sources deemed by BlackRock to be reliable, are not necessarily all-inclusive and are not guaranteed as to accuracy.

BlackRock provides compensation in connection with obtaining or using third-party ratings and rankings.

1 Class R shares are sold to a limited group of investors, including certain retirement plans. See prospectus for details. 2 The Russell Midcap Growth Index comprises mid-capitalization

U.S. equities that exhibit growth characteristics. Securities in this index generally have higher price-to-book and price-to-earnings ratios, lower dividend yields and higher forecasted growth values.

You should consider the investment objectives, risks, charges and expenses of the fund carefully before investing. The prospectus and, if available, the summary prospectus contain this and other information about the fund and are available, along with information on other BlackRock funds, by calling 800-882-0052 or from your financial professional. The prospectus should be read carefully before investing.

data.title: Twin Hospitality's FAT Brands Liquidity Falls As Preferred Dividends Suspended
data.description:
FAT Brands (NASDAQ:FAT) (NASDAQ:FATBB) suspended its quarterly dividend to common shareholders at the start of 2025, following up with a suspension of its monthly paid distribution to its preferred shareholders (NASDAQ:FATBP) a few months later. I warned about the looming cuts in my prior coverage of the ticker. Fundamentally, the distributions were unsustainable for a company that ended its fiscal 2025 first quarter with long-term debt of $1.2 billion, a roughly 23x multiple of its current market and far in excess of cash, cash equivalents, and restricted cash of $41.5 million as of the end of the quarter. FAT ended the quarter with a total stockholders' deficit of $500 million, up $45 million sequentially, as its total liabilities reached $1.77 billion against total assets of $1.27 billion. With total assets 68.8% constituted by goodwill and intangible assets, the liquidation value of FAT's balance sheet, as per a crude total assets minus goodwill, could be much lower. FAT completed its spinoff of its Twin Peaks and Smokey Bones restaurant chain (NASDAQ:TWNP) back in January but retains 95% ownership in the publicly traded entity. The performance of FAT is invariably interlocked with the performance of TWNP.

FAT is unprofitable, burning through its dwindling cash reserve, and faces a mountain of debt that threatens to force the company into tough questions around how to possibly restructure its debt. FAT generated first quarter revenue of $142.01 million, down 6.6% over its year-ago comp and a miss by $6.54 million on consensus estimates. Net income was negative at $46 million, up from $38.3 million a year ago. FAT trades on a 0.085x multiple to its annualized first quarter revenue at its current market cap.

Twin Peaks Performance

TWNP generated first-quarter revenue of $87.1 million, down 5.4% over its year-ago comp, and with a net loss of $12.1 million growing from $9.2 million a year ago. TWNP currently trades on a $302 million market cap, around a 0.87x price-to-sales multiple to annualized first quarter revenue. The market seems to have extended an olive branch to TWNP in terms of its valuation despite the company suffering from the same heavy debt burden, falling liquidity position, and negative free cash flow that FAT has seen its commons get weighed down by. The company's interest expenses at $10.8 million represent a core headwind, as long-term debt stood at $413 million as of the end of the first quarter. Cash and cash equivalents were $7.15 million, with free cash burn during the quarter at $9.7 million. TWNP will likely have to lean on equity sales to make up its liquidity gap.

FAT's Twin Peaks Securitization Notes, Liquidity, And Free Cash Burn

FAT held $402.3 million of Twin Peaks securitization notes at book value as of the end of the first quarter. These are secured by a security interest in substantially all the assets of FAT Brands Twin Peaks I, a subsidiary created in connection with the October 2021 acquisition of Twin Peaks. FAT currently pays a coupon of 7% on 69.05% of the aggregate TWNP securitization notes. Critically, while the legal final maturity date of the notes is October 26, 2054, the anticipated repayment date is much sooner on October 25, 2027. Why? If the notes are not repaid or refinanced by the anticipated repayment date, then FAT will face additional interest on the outstanding balance of each tranche of the notes at a rate of 5% per year. Further, TWNP is required to deposit 75% of the net proceeds of each qualified equity offering of its common shares into a segregated account to be used for repayment of the notes until an aggregate of $75 million has been repaid. TWNP currently has a market cap of $302 million, less than the aggregate number of notes owed by FAT.

FAT is now in a position where is operations are fundamentally unprofitable, with the unrestricted portion of its cash position in free fall. This cash position was $12.15 million as of the end of the first quarter, amongst its lowest level in over 3 years, as quarterly free cash flow notched another negative quarter at $17.76 million. FAT has made up this liquidity gap in the past by adding more debt, with its total long-term debt up 40% over the last 3 years. This becomes increasingly unsustainable the further the equity component of the company declines.

Negative shareholders' equity has deepened further as near-term liquidity transactions threaten to ramp up this trend. FAT has access to $73.6 million from issued but not sold fixed-rate secured notes and $75.6 million from repurchased but not resold fixed-rate secured notes. The company considers the proceeds from these to be sufficient for operations through the next 12 months. There will still have to be underlying investor demand for these.

What is the upside and risk to the bearish thesis here? TWNP is able to refinance its debt at favourable rates and is also able to reduce cash burn from current levels. While these risks, will not reinstate the common share or preferred dividends in the near to medium term. They could improve sentiment on both. The preferreds have collapsed to $3.60 per share following the dividend suspension, around 14 cents on the dollar. The company is effectively burning through the last of its liquidity sources as it records consecutive quarters of negative free cash flow. I don't see these as having any latent recovery value. FAT and TWNP both remain a sell.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: NCR Voyix: Weak Revenue Trend Continues
data.description:
NCR Voyix Corporation (VYX) reported stronger-than-expected Q1 results on the 8th of May. The restaurant and retail software provider is still seeing weak growth in recurring revenues, though, and hardware revenues are also declining sharply prior to the Ennoconn outsourcing deal being implemented. As a silver lining, NCR Voyix’s profitability is benefiting from 2024 cost actions.

In my previous December 2024 article on the stock, “NCR Voyix's Best-Performing Segment Is Being Sold”, I downgraded my rating to Sell as the Digital Banking segment got divested. The stock has since returned -7%, well below S&P 500’s return of 12%.

NCR Voyix’s Q1 Sales Trends Aren’t Encouraging Despite the Beat

NCR Voyix’s Q1 revenues reflected continued trends in the company’s performance. Total revenues declined by -13% to $617 million, driven by a significant decline in hardware revenues, and a more moderate decline in service and software revenues. Despite sharp weakness, the topline result still beat Wall Street’s weak consensus estimate by $12 million.

The restaurants segment showed around a -6% software & service revenue decline to $105 million, and the critical ARR metric declined -1% for the segment. While NCR Voyix relates to a good number of new customers in the Q1 earnings call, including Ziggi’s Coffee with 150 locations, a renewal and expansion with Raising Cane’s, and an expansion with Buffalo Wild Wings, recurring revenues don’t seem to be following the good amount of signed new customers and growth in total payment and platform sites.

The retail segment’s underlying performance was slightly better. While combined software & service revenues also declined for the retail segment by -5% to $324 million, critically, ARR grew by 4%. New customer growth contributed towards good momentum in the segment, where recurring revenue growth only decelerated slightly from 6% in Q4.

Both segments’ significant hardware sales decline isn’t too concerning; maintaining healthy recurring software and service revenues is way more critical for NCR Voyix. Hardware sales declines follow a clear declining trend from previous years, where NCR Voyix’s hardware sales, for example, declined by -27% in 2024. Similarly, Q1 hardware sales declined by -29% in Q1 to $138 million. NCR Voyix previously signed an agreement with Ennoconn to switch the hardware business to an outsourced model during 2025, where Ennoconn will soon take over the responsibility for hardware sales. NCR Voyix will still generate commission revenues under the new model, but the business model shift still clearly switches NCR Voyix’s focus point increasingly on recurring revenues.

NCR Voyix kept the 2025 software and service revenue guidance unchanged at $1995-2020, million, suggesting a -2.3% decline at the mid-point; the underlying sales outlook remains subdued, suggesting that quite weak recurring sales trends will continue past Q1. Better ARR growth in the retail segment doesn't seem to salvage the total revenue performance.

Profitability Benefits from Cost Actions

While sales declined, NXR Voyix’s adjusted EBITDA reached $75 million, up 19% year-on-year through 330 basis point expansion in the EBITDA margin. NCR Voyix’s 2024 cost actions, including significant restructuring on corporate expenses and other efficiency initiatives, yielded significant results against a decline in combined segment EBITDA. NCR is still doing restructuring in 2025, but to a significantly lower degree. The reaffirmed 2025 guidance suggests great margin expansion for the full year. NCR anticipates adjusted EBITDA at $420-445 million, up significantly from $347 million in 2024. The EBITDA margin is guided at 16.3% to 16.8%.

I believe that the expected expansion in earnings is a good silver lining, as NCR Voyix’s revenues are projected to continue declining. Ultimately, a sales trend improvement is more critical over the long term; long-term earnings growth isn’t achieved with cost cuts.

Revenue Concerns Make VYX’s Valuation Unappealing

I updated my discounted cash flow (DCF) model for NCR Voyix’s stock. I now estimate adjusted EBITDA profitability instead of GAAP EBIT.

I assume 2025 revenues to come in at $2600 million, within NCR Voyix’s guidance range. Inow estimate 2026 revenues to decline to $2100 million based on slow organic growth and the Ennoconn hardware outsourcing deal being implemented during 2025. Afterwards, I now expect subdued 1% perpetual growth given NCR Voyix’s slow performance in recurring revenues.

Through restructuring and the outsourcing deal, I expect NCR Voyix’s EBITDA margin to reach 22%, suggesting some total EBITDA growth for 2026 and a stable margin performance afterwards.

Cash flow will be weak for 2025 due to the Digital Banking segment’s divestment-related tax payment of $284 million in Q2. Afterwards, I expect cash flow to be healthy, but to be weakened by NCR Voyix’s significant amount of capitalized development expenses.

The estimates put NCR Voyix’s stock’s fair value at $9.31, 14% below the stock price at the time of writing; the stock remains unattractive for now. Although cost cuts have improved the cash flow profile for now, the low growth outlook makes the stock unappealing at the current price. Better ARR growth could make the stock attractive, but I don’t expect good growth to materialize as a base scenario.

The fair value estimate is up from $8.54 previously.

CAPM

The DCF model uses a cost of capital [WACC] of 10.09%, derived from a capital asset pricing model:

I estimate a 5.46% interest rate, annualized from Q1 interest expenses and debt. As NCR Voyix has decided to keep a good amount of debt even after the Digital Banking divestment, I now estimate a significantly higher 30% long-term debt-to-equity ratio.

For cost of equity, I use the 10-year bond yield of 4.49% as the risk-free rate. The equity risk premium of 4.33% is Professor Aswath Damodaran’s most recent January estimates for the US. I have kept the beta estimate at 1.65. With a liquidity premium of 0.25%, the cost of equity stands at 11.88% and the WACC at 10.09%.

Takeaway

NCR Voyix’s recurring revenues are slightly declining, causing a weak outlook after the Digital Banking sale. Hardware sales are declining even more sharply, but less concerningly, due to the upcoming Ennoconn outsourcing implementation. NCR Voyix’s profitability is benefiting from significant cost actions, but restructuring ultimately isn’t a viable way to raise the long-term earnings growth outlook against weakening revenues. I still estimate the stock’s valuation to be quite unappealing given the growth outlook, and remain at a Sell rating for NCR Voyix.

data.title: Floating Beats Flailing
data.description:
Wall Street kept its head above water Thursday, paddling through oil-country rumors, retail grumbles, and supply shock whispers. The Nasdaq paused for breath, but the rest of the market managed a modest rise despite a fresh round of tariff Tetris for corporate America.

Level Change 5/15/25 (%)

+0.7 Dow

-0.2 Nasdaq

+0.1 Nasdaq 100

+0.4 S&P 500

+0.2 S&P 400

+0.6 S&P 600

Stocks are trading like last month’s panic was just a fever dream. The S&P 500 is within 4% of record highs, and the Nasdaq 100 has pole-vaulted from bear to bull. Easing trade tensions and a White House that sounds less like it’s spoiling for a fight are giving buyers reason to keep pressing the gas.

And today, mixed data, middling earnings, and muted Fedspeak left equities to interpret their own tea leaves. They brewed a mild blend.

Bonds reminded everyone who’s boss, and gave permission for stocks to stretch their legs. Investors exhaled as Treasury yields edged lower. The 10-year yield fell 10 basis points to 4.43%, easing pressure on equities after flirting with 4.50% earlier in the week. It wasn’t a breakout, but it was a break.

Retail sales flubbed the forecast across the board - headline, core, control group all underwhelmed. March was revised higher, softening the sting, but April showed that shoppers didn’t just pause, they RSVP’d no.

However, core PPI surprised to the downside in its biggest monthly drop in five years, thanks to sagging wholesale margins on machinery and vehicles. Not bad news, suggesting as it does that inflation’s still curled up somewhere in the supply chain, hitting snooze.

Initial jobless claims ticked up a bit less than feared, but continuing claims moved higher. Industrial production flatlined, the Empire State survey flopped, and the NAHB housing index sank to a six-month low. The Philly Fed missed, but not by much - an achievement in a world where “less disappointing” counts as bullish.

Fed Chair Powell skipped rate talk Thursday, preferring the safer pastures of long-term strategy tweaks and a renewed love letter to the 2% inflation target.

He warned the economy may be entering a stretch of frequent supply shocks, a theme he’s repeated in recent weeks. Tariffs didn’t make the script this time, but Powell and others have made clear: they expect tariffs to slow growth and stoke inflation - just the kind of balancing tightrope central bankers dream about.

Corporate highlights offered a study in contrasts.

Walmart (WMT -0.5%) beat earnings and stuck to its full-year guidance, but warned that even it can’t outmuscle tariffs forever. “We will do our best to keep our prices as low as possible,” said CEO Doug McMillon, but tariffs don’t shop at Walmart.

April and May have already seen sticker creep. By June, said CFO John David Rainey, shopping carts will bear the full weight of a trade policy even its architects can’t quite explain.

About 15% of Walmart’s products come from China - the tariff minefield - while 60% are groceries, mostly tariff-proof thanks to domestic and North American sourcing. Unfortunately, canned corn doesn’t offset a $50 toaster.

Cisco (CSCO +4.9%) beat estimates and talked up AI momentum: customers are spending big on security and software, but didn’t panic-order ahead of tariff hits.

Sure, maybe a client here or there pulled the trigger early, said CEO Chuck Robbins, “But we looked at a ton of data points to see if we saw any signs of broad-based pull-ahead business, and we did not.” In this economy, AI spending is right up there with bread and milk.

Meanwhile, in Qatar, Trump took time to scold Apple (AAPL -0.4%) for shifting iPhone production from China to India in a bid to sidestep tariffs. Build them in America, he urged, apparently unfazed by the $3,500 price tag such patriotism would require.

That’s Wedbush’s estimate for a US-made model. Apple CEO Tim Cook may be Trump’s friend, but he’s not suicidal. Triple the price, and even Apple fans might start making eye contact again.

And in coffee news, Starbucks (SBUX +0.8%) is reportedly considering selling part of its struggling China business, according to Bloomberg. The Seattle siren has been out-foamed by local champs like Luckin (OTCPK:LKNCY) and Chagee (CHA), which have now set their sights on US shores. America, gird your loins: the next great trade war may be fought in tapioca pearls and lavender cold brew.

No blowouts, no breakdowns. Just a market inching through muted data, cautious guidance, and the drip-drip of tariff fallout. Some days, treading water is a win.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: VDY: This Canadian Dividend ETF Is Leaving SCHD In The Dust
data.description:
Continuing on the path of my recent Seeking Alpha coverage of foreign stock opportunities for American investors, this article will focus on the Canadian High Dividend Yield ETF (TSX:VDY:CA)(OTC:VDYIF). Back in December 2022, I started my coverage of the VDY ETF with a SELL rating due to its significant under-performance as compared to the Schwab U.S. Dividend ETF (SCHD). However, times change - and so do foreign currency exchange rates, foreign policies, and total returns. Indeed, over the past year, the VDY ETF has outperformed SCHD by 14%+ (see chart below). That being the case, today I will take a fresh look at VDY to see if it may now represent a more compelling total returns opportunity and, if so, adjust my rating accordingly.

Note: For simplicity's sake, for the rest of this article I will use "VDY" in place of VDY:CA or VDY:TO and use $C or CAD in place of "the Canadian dollar".

Investment Thesis

Most of my followers are likely already quite familiar with my opinion on the current Trump administration's foreign & economic policies. After all, Seeking Alpha published two of my articles that are quite critical of Trump's policies (see The 4-Fund Trump Portfolio and American Exceptionalism: Gone In 90-Days). I won't repeat the arguments (well-referenced, mind you ...) made in those articles other than to point out that, despite the recent (and not finalized ...) tariff "wins" with the UK and China, the U.S. dollar index is still down significantly YTD while the CAD exchange rate against the U.S. dollar is up significantly:

What that means for investors is that the value of Canadian dividends are simply worth much more than they were prior to Trump taking office. And this simple fact goes a long-way toward explaining the 14%+ out-performance of the VDY ETF versus the SCHD ETF because roughly half of that out-performance is just since Trump took office less than 4-months ago.

As outlined in the two articles referenced earlier, the U.S. dollar has likely entered into a period of enduring weakness due to the Trump administration's foreign policies as well, and it is important to understand the impact those foreign policies can have on the status and health of the U.S. And, if it does turn out that the U.S. dollar is in secular long-term decline, ETFs focused on Canadian dividend paying stocks will turn out to be excellent investments for the American investor.

With that as background, I'll now take a closer look at the VDY ETF, starting with its portfolio.

Top-10 Holdings

The top-10 holdings in the VDY ETF (as of March 30, 2025) are shown below and were taken directly from the Vanguard VDY webpage where you can find more detailed information on the fund:

As you can see from the graphic, and what most investors familiar with the Canadian stock market likely expect, the VDY ETF is most heavily exposed to Canada's Financial and Energy sectors. The fund is also moderately concentrated, with the top-10 holdings equating to ~66% of the entire portfolio.

The top holding in the VDY ETF is the Royal Bank of Canada (RY) (RY:CA) with a 13.84% weight. The Toronto-based bank currently trades with a forward P/E of only 12.8x and yields 3.41%. In March, Seeking Alpha contributor David Trainer rated RY a BUY because of its attractive risk/reward profile.

Enbridge (ENB) is the #3 holding with an 8.4% weight. Enbridge is the largest midstream company in North America and also runs a number of natural gas utility companies in Canada and the U.S. The stock was fairly stagnant for the better part of a decade but over the past year, it has been on a very strong run as the $C rallied against the US$, making ENB's now 6.0% dividend yield very attractive:

The #5 holding is Canadian Natural Resources (CNQ) with a 5.5% weight. In my opinion, CNQ is the class of the Canadian oil sands patch and has delivered superior 10-year full-cycle total returns as compared to its peers Suncor (SU) and Cenovus (CVE):

A slide from CNQ's most recent presentation informs investors that it is the only Canadian energy company with more total proved reserves of 5+ billion boe and has the most crude oil and the most natural gas proved reserves of any company in Canada (a massive 32-year reserve life, roughly 2x of its peers):

CNQ currently trades with a forward P/E of only 13.8x and yields 4.88%.

The #9 holding is Canadian pipeline operator TC Energy (TRP) with a 4.3% weight. TRP focuses primarily on operating natural gas pipelines in Canada, the U.S., and Mexico. The stock yields 5.0%.

The top-10 holdings are rounded out by the aforementioned Suncor with a 4.2% weight. Suncor yields 4.39% and - like CNQ and Cenovus - should be a beneficiary of the Transmountain Pipeline Expansion project, which went in-service in May of last year and is now, finally, providing additional and critical oil sands takeaway capacity to reach global markets via the West Coast of Canada.

For the portfolio as a whole, the VDY ETF is very heavily exposed to only two sectors (82%):

Further, in my opinion, the health of Canadian banks is likely highly correlated to the health of Canada's energy companies. So, this over-concentration is certainly a concern of mine.

Performance

The chart below shows the VDY ETF's long-term performance track record:

As you can see from the graphic, VDY's 5-year average total returns have been quite strong, while the 10-year and since inception returns are solid.

The graphic below compares the 3-year total returns of the VDY ETF with some of its dividend competitors (both in Canada and the U.S.): the iShares Core MSCI Canadian Quality Dividend Index ETF (XDIV:CA), the Vanguard Dividend Appreciation Index Fund ETF (VIG), the Vanguard High Dividend Yield Index ETF (VYM), and SCHD:

As you can see, over the past three years the results are mixed, but clearly the XDIV has outperformed them all, with SCHD pulling up the bottom.

Dividends

VDY pays a monthly dividend (currently $0.18/unit) and the TTM yield is 4.17%. The chart below shows the 5-year annual dividend payment track-record:

As you can, VDY's dividend growth has been lumpy, but strong overall and with a big "lump" in 2022 ($3.18/unit) following Putin's invasion of Ukraine that drove oil to ~$120/bbl and was a windfall for the Canadian oil & gas producers.

Basic ETF Metrics

The following are some basic ETF metrics from the VDY webpage referenced earlier and that most all investors would be interested in prior to possibly investing into the fund:

  • AUM: C$3.5 billion
  • Class: High Dividend Yield Equity Fund
  • Base Currency: CAD
  • Exchange: Toronto Stock Exchange ("TSE")
  • Management Expense Ratio: 0.22%
  • # Holdings: 63
  • TTM Yield: 4.17%
  • Distribution Frequency: Monthly (currently $0.18/unit)
  • P/E Ratio: 15.6x
  • P/B Ratio: 1.7x
  • ROE: 11.9%

So, this is a relatively well-subscribed fund with assets-under-management of C$3.5 billion. The expense ratio of 0.22% is higher than I typically like to pay, but with 63 holdings, it is more diversified (at least company wise, but not sector wise ...) than other dividend focused Canadian funds I am familiar with (see XDIV reference below). The valuation of VDY is roughly half that of the S&P500 and the yield is attractive and significantly more than triple that of the S&P500 (1.27%).

Risks

Canadian companies could be severely and negatively impacted should a rational tariff policy agreement between the U.S. and Canada not be reached. That said, it is my opinion that if that turns out to be the case, U.S. companies may likely be just as negatively impacted (if not more so) than Canadian companies.

Canadian companies are also not immune from the impact of the U.S. and global economies and the macroeconomic environment in general. As I mentioned in my previous article on the XDIV ETF: the old saying is, "if the U.S. sneezes, the rest of the world catches a cold."

For American investors, the VDY ETF is directly impacted by foreign exchange fluctuations between the US$ and the CAD. Americans can buy shares of VDY on the Toronto exchange, but make sure to check your broker's currency translation before you put in an order to make sure you are getting a fair-shake (i.e. the current exchange rate) on the trade with no extra "fees" that can directly inflate your cost-basis. That is, like XDIV, it is my understanding that American investors can only buy the VDY ETF on the TSE using $C.

Summary & Profile

I still like the general thesis that American investors should be exposed to foreign-currency based dividend paying companies to protect themselves from the potential of a continually weakening U.S. dollar. On that basis, as well as the strong yield and solid long-term performance track record, I raise the VDY ETF from SELL to BUY.

That said, and despite my typical bias toward Vanguard's high-quality low-cost ETFs (the VOO ETF is the foundation of my portfolio), I am going to have to go with the XDIV:CA ETF when it comes to a Canadian focused dividend ETF. Despite the fact that VDY is much more diversified (63 holdings versus XDIV's 40), VDY's expense fee is 2x that of XDIV and while the 5-year and 10-year total returns are roughly identical, the 3-year total returns give a significant edge to XDIV:

If interested in the XDIV ETF, please consider reading my recent Seeking Alpha article: XDIV: Suddenly, Canadian Dividend Paying Stocks Look Fantastic.

Yet, as I said, I rate both VDY and XDIV as BUYs here. However, the weakening U.S. dollar thesis really needs to play out for these two funds to thrive and continue to outperform. I say that because perhaps the biggest risk investors in these Canadian funds face is the longer-term track record that clearly shows both funds significantly under-performing American funds in both the broad market averages (VOO), (QQQ), (DIA), and a U.S. oriented dividend fund like SCHD. A picture is worth a thousand words in this regard, so I will end with a 10-year total returns comparison of all these funds:

data.title: Berkshire Hathaway's Q1 13F Filing Reveals Buffett's Thinking About Bank of America And Apple
data.description:
Today's May 15 13F Filing is quite revealing of how Buffett feels about banks, particularly Bank of America (BAC), as well as his current thinking about Apple (AAPL). The Apple revelation was simple, so let's start there. His Apple holding of 300,000,000 shares was the same as it was on December 31, 2024, meaning that he didn't stop selling just to keep his promise about Apple's position as his number one stock holding until the end of the year. He had reduced his position in Apple because his previous Apple holdings were out of scale to the portfolio as a whole. It was position-sizing pure and simple.

Quite a few things go into position-sizing, of course. If the future had been clear and the valuation had been, perhaps, half what it was, he might well have been satisfied with the enormous Apple holding which he had previously treated as Berkshire's (NYSE:BRK.A)(NYSE:BRK.B) fourth major business. What he had to take into account was that Apple's valuation was about 150% of what it was when he started buying it. That meant that 60% of his gain from that initial purchase was pure valuation increase. That made Apple particularly vulnerable and large enough that if the market fell sharply on the basis of valuation, Apple would fall hard with it and inflict serious damage upon Berkshire's overall value. There's also the matter of selling a large chunk of it and paying the cap gains taxes now while the corporate tax rate, very likely to go up in the future, was only 21%. Instead of a huge liability, which Buffett obviously saw, he was able to turn his Apple gains into a T-Bill bulwark against whatever might happen in the market.

None of that meant that Buffett did not still appreciate Apple's business. Apple still constitutes 26% of the Berkshire publicly traded portfolio, or did at the end of Q1 on March 31. Nevertheless, the probability of rapid growth, though not zero, were greatly reduced in part because of its size and the absence of new products likely to move the needle. The more probable outcome, he most likely decided, was that Apple would continue to have wonderful cash flow and settle into a future with strong dividends and modest growth like American Express (AXP) which he has held through thick and thin. Never mind the ups and downs of the share price, just focus on future cash returns. As a part of the public portfolio, Apple still was a position 10% larger than the company in second place, possibly because it is earlier in its life cycle.

All of the above projections of Buffett's thinking, of course, came before the full impact of tariffs on Apple started to become clear and far in advance of Trump's reported pressure on Apple to bring its production home to the U.S. instead of building factories all over India. This problem may be worked out by some part of compromise, although if fully executed the moving to domestic plants could raise the cost of iPhones from $1000 to $3500 according to analyst Dan Ives, a leading tech analyst at Wedbush Securities. Ives described the difficulties of building new plants in the U.S. as so difficult and costly that actually trying to do it seemed out of the question. Awareness of this new challenge may or may not shift the probabilities in Buffett's mind. In any case, the Trump message to Tim Cook was enough to threaten to sink Apple's stock. Buffett must be of a divided mind about that, glad he sold what he sold, worried a bit about what he kept.

Bank of America Is a Very Different Story

The short version: Buffett continued to sell Bank of America, reducing Berkshire's position from 680 billion shares to 631,575,531. That's an almost 50 billion reduction in the share count, and it seems likely that Buffett dumped BAC about as fast as possible in the first quarter without having a huge impact on the market. This continued selling also suggests that he may not be done with reducing his BAC position. In other words, it may not be driven so much by position-sizing as by the large number of challenges to banks in general. What reinforces this thought is the fact that he entirely eliminated the much smaller position in Citigroup (C) and modestly reduced the position in Capital One Financial (COF).

With Bank of America there's the same capital gains problem as with Apple because its average cost is around $14 per share, or roughly a third of its present price, meaning that at the current price in the low 40s cap gains at 21% would have to be paid on two thirds of the position (again there is the general assumption that the corporate tax rate will rise in the future regardless of the party in power). Another issue is the increase in interest rates which reduces the value of present fixed income in bank portfolios, now combined with the possibility of a recession at the same time impacting net interest income. Additionally, banks have ongoing problems with fintech, the question of whether to improve or reduce branches, and the fact that banks continue to live under the cloud of the last financial crisis and are an easy target for politicians.

There is one more interesting aspect of the heavy selling of BAC, a detail easy to pass over. At the end of 2024, Apple was still Berkshire's number one holding, as it continues to be today, with BAC at number two. After the heavy selling of the first quarter, however, the ranking on March 31st had Bank of America dropping from 2d to fourth behind American Express and Coca-Cola (KO). AXP was in second place at 16% of Berkshire's total portfolio, while KO was second at 11% and BAC third at 10%. The reason this matters is that with more selling likely to come, BAC seems to be losing the argument that Buffett just wanted to reduce it to a perpetual dividend stock like American Express and Coke. Instead, it raises the question as to whether Buffett sees a bank like Bank of America as a suitable prospect for continuing modest growth and a slightly rising dividend. The risks, one might speculate, strike Buffett as too great to think of it as a stable dividend provider.

Much of the above arguments are projective of what Buffett may be thinking, but the underlying reasons are rational and likely although, of course, there are wrinkles in Buffett's analysis which can easily include second or third order thinking. There was of course, other buying and selling in the 13F filing, but the two discussed above are the ones with revelations about Buffett's overall thinking.

data.title: GCC Advances In Odessa With Risks On The Oil Market
data.description:
GCC (OTCPK:GCWOF) reported 1Q25 earnings in late April, with results that disappointed a little in terms of sales volumes and margins, but that had difficult comps in 1Q24.

The company is expecting little changes to guidance and markets in FY25. I see the main risk coming from the oil market in the US, particularly as the company expects to bring the Odessa expansion online in 2H25.

In terms of valuation, the name trades at a slightly lower market cap than in my last report, from February 2025, in which I considered that a 10x earnings multiple on cyclical high earnings was not attractive. With a valuation now at about 8x cycle-high earnings, but more risks pointing towards a cycle reversal, I maintain my Hold rating.

1Q25 results by segment

Volumes continue down

The main data point to extract from the earnings release and the call was that volumes continue to decrease. In this case, cement volumes in the US were down 4.3,% and volumes in Mexico were down 12.7%

On the one hand, there were some climatic and exogenous factors involved. In the US, last year's winter was pretty mild, and therefore allowed for more activity in the Central and Mountain regions, where GCC has its operational base. In Mexico, excessive winds delayed operations for more than two weeks in two of the company's plants, therefore affecting outputs.

Still, we have to remember that cement volumes were down most of FY24, with some moderation in 4Q24. The 1Q25 worsening trend, if continued, will eventually affect pricing.

Pricing remains very strong

Despite the above, the second factor in determining GCC's profitability, pricing, was very good. In the US, cement and concrete prices rose 3% and 12.1% YoY, respectively. In Mexico, cement prices also rose, 5% in this case. This is a sign of market health because in a market with excess capacity, the ability of producers to increase prices would be more limited.

Worsening margins via volume deleverage

The problem with the cement cycle is that if GCC sells lower volumes, the fixed costs (plant CAPEX, employees, overhead, and administration) are spread over fewer tons of cement, therefore affecting the company's margins. If prices were to turn a negative factor, this would deteriorate even more.

1Q25 was a sign of what can happen when volumes decrease significantly, with gross margins down 240 bps. This translated only to 60bps of EBITDA margin decrease because of SG&A decreases (40bps) plus the relevant effect of non-cash D&A on CoGS.

End market analysis

The company has three main markets in the US (energy, housing, and infrastructure) and two main markets in Mexico (industrial and housing).

Starting with Mexico, the industrial market remains oversupplied, meaning there is less construction activity. This is the main driver of falling sales in the country. Housing is doing better (no mentions in Q1, but mentions in 4Q24's call). Mexican demand is very dependent on US investment in manufacturing, and therefore on the US investment and economic cycle because the company's facilities are all in the Mexican North.

In the US, I would not think that things are looking better for housing and private infrastructure investment after the tariff chaos. The risk of a recession is higher, even after the US-China agreements, and therefore, investment is probably more cautious. The company is optimistic about the implementation of IIJA, with more than 2/3s of funding unallocated.

The main risk I see is in US oil production. GCC's four largest US plants are in Colorado, New Mexico, Texas, and South Dakota, which, except South Dakota (replaced by North Dakota), are the largest oil producers in the US. Further, the company is about to triple the capacity in its Odessa, TX plant, which is deep in Permian Basin country.

The risk I see is that a decrease in drilling activity hits GCC's volumes and margins, at a time when it is bringing more capacity online (and therefore is in the highest need of volumes) in Odessa.

When the earnings call happened, oil was already in the $65 range, and management said that US production could continue in the low 60s. The latest drilling data from early May are negative, but it is still very early to call a trend.

Valuation remains unattractive

GCC now trades at about 10% lower valuation ($3 billion versus $3.3 billion) than when I last wrote about it in February 2025.

Back then, I considered the expansion potential from Odessa, plus cycle-dependent margins to arrive at a forecast of revenues and NOPATs of $1.7 billion and $360 million for bull periods (like the one experienced now) and $1.55 billion and $220 million for the cycle average.

Compared to these figures, the company's market cap is 13x cycle-average adjusted earnings, and about 8.3x for current, positive environment periods.

Considering that there are increasing signs of a deceleration in the cycle, and that the company will open Odessa in a potentially hard oil climate, I believe these multiples are not super attractive. I prefer to remain on the sidelines tactically, waiting for more attractive prices to present later. For that reason, I maintain a Hold rating.

Still, I continue to believe GCC is one of the best-managed cement companies I have reviewed, and that it is a quality company. For that reason, some might consider it attractive as a buy and hold alternative, taking a long-term view that is independent of the current position in the cycle.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Inditex: Cyclical Slowdown Not Likely To Reverse Soon
data.description:
As I’ve covered in a previous article, while I see Industria de Diseno Textil – Inditex (OTCPK:IDEXY) as an interesting growth play in the retail sector over the long term, its full valuation and moderating growth were two major concerns some months ago and therefore my recommendation on its shares was ‘hold’ at the time. Since then, its shares have traded sideways but have beaten slightly the market during the same period, showing that upside potential was indeed limited, at least in the short term.

A couple of months ago, the company released its Fiscal Year (FY) 2024 earnings, reporting slower sales growth and earnings slightly below expectations, justifying its relatively weak share price momentum in recent months.

Indeed, Inditex’s revenues amounted to €38.6 billion in the last fiscal year, up by about 7.5% compared to the previous one. While this is still an acceptable growth rate, it’s much lower than compared to its strong operating momentum following the pandemic and has been on a declining trend in recent quarters, being concerning for a ‘growth’ company like Inditex. This slower growth was also penalized by currency moves, which had an impact of about three percentage points on sales growth, which is quite significant considering that about 65% of the company’s sales are generated in Europe.

By brand, all segments reported higher revenues during the last fiscal year, but its flagship brand Zara reported sales growth of 6.6% YoY, being an underperformer within the group, while brands like Stradivarius and Massimo Dutti had better performances with annual sales growth above 20%.

After the end of FY 2024, Inditex has launched the spring/summer collection and sales growth between the beginning of February to March 10 th was only 4%, showing that the deceleration of sales growth has not reversed in the first weeks of FY 2025, raising concerns about the company’s growth outlook in the short term. This is the weakest growth since the pandemic and suggests a cyclical slowdown justified by external issues, namely weak economic growth across Europe and some weakness in Asia and the Americas, rather than, in my opinion, some fundamental issues about its business model.

This also reflects some growth normalization following a strong growth period following the pandemic, which was not sustainable, and Inditex is now returning to a more ‘normal’ sales growth path of 6-8% on an annual basis. Indeed, Inditex’s long-term guidance is to achieve 4-6% like-for-like sales growth, plus add about 2% sales growth from new space, which means its sales growth in FY 2024 was not much different than what the company expects to achieve over the long term.

Beyond sales growth, other important financial metrics reported strong figures in FY 2024, showing that Inditex is doing a good job to improving operating efficiency and protecting its business margins. Showing that despite slowing sales growth its pricing has remained robust, Inditex’s gross margin was 57.8% in FY 2024, representing an improvement of 8 basis points (bps) compared to the previous year. Regarding operating expenses, the company also did a good job to control cost growth, leading to overall expenses of €11.5 billion (+6.5% YoY), which increased less than sales growth during the past fiscal year. This means the company was able to slightly improve its operating leverage, reaching a new record operating profit margin of 19.6%, ten basis points above its previous record high achieved in FY 2013.

Despite this positive operating performance, there were some factors suggesting that recent growth slowdown is not likely to reverse soon, namely inventories increased by 12% YoY to more than €3.3 billion at the end of FY 2024, representing more than 31 days of sales. While this is a small difference compared to the end of the previous FY, higher inventories is not a good signal about consumer demand for its products, especially during a period in which Inditex is making significant investments in logistics.

Indeed, due to strong sales growth over the past few years, Inditex decided to make a large investment on logistics in Spain and the Netherlands, as the company was quite confident about consumer demand for its brands over the medium to long term. This is putting some pressure on the company’s free cash flow generation given that capital expenditures amounted to more than €2.6 billion in FY 2024, up by 42% YoY.

Because of this larger investment in logistics, its free cash flow declined over the last year despite the company reaching record earnings, but the decline was only 5% from the previous year to €4.8 billion. This was a good outcome considering the significant capex increase, a trend that is expected to be maintained during 2025, as Inditex’s investments on logistics should be spread between the two fiscal years. Indeed, according to analysts’ estimates, Inditex’s capex is expected to be about €2.7 billion in FY 2025, but decline to a more ‘normal’ level of about €2 billion in FY 2026, while free cash flow should return to a growing path thereafter.

Regarding the company’s balance sheet position, it remained very strong as Inditex had a net cash position of €11.5 billion at the end of last fiscal year and practically don’t have any financial debt. This net cash position represents close to 8% of its current market value of about €148 billion, showing that Inditex’s cash position is quite large, and its balance sheet is clearly strong.

This strong balance sheet and the company’s high profitability levels are clearly supportive of a growing dividend over the next few years, even though Inditex is somewhat conservative and is only targeting a dividend payout ratio of 60%, plus some bonus dividend payments. This seems somewhat low for a company that has very strong fundamentals and does not need to retain much cash, but Inditex’s ordinary dividend growth is likely to remain similar to earnings growth ahead, while special dividends are likely in the future as the company does not need to retain earnings due to a strong balance sheet position.

Related to FY 2024 earnings, Inditex increased its dividend by 9% YoY to €1.68 per share including both ordinary and special dividends, a growth rate that was quite close to earnings growth in the year. At its current share price leads to a dividend yield of about 3.5%. This is an interesting yield for a growth company like Inditex and is above-average compared to other companies in the apparel industry, showing that its profile is switching from growth to a more mature company and, potentially, income may become an important factor of its investment case over the coming years.

Having said that, I think most investors still see Inditex as a growth play and the company’s guidance is to grow gross store space at about 5% per year during 2025-26, being an import support for the company to reach sales growth in the high-single digits over the coming years. On the other hand, the trade war and new tariffs in the U.S. may be a headwind for sales growth in the short term, even though Inditex’s exposure to the U.S. is not much large, but it can be an important setback for higher sales growth ahead.

Regarding its valuation, its shares have de-rated somewhat in recent months as business growth has moderated and investors have increasingly become more concerned about Inditex’s growth prospects in the short term. Indeed, Inditex is currently trading at about 23x forward earnings, slightly cheaper than compared to when I last covered it (24.4x forward earnings at the time). This multiple is now closer to its historical average over the past five years, but investors should be aware that Inditex usually trades between 20-25x forward earnings. Thus I still don’t see its shares as being undervalued right now.

Conclusion

Inditex has strong fundamentals and positive growth prospects over the long term, but it’s currently suffering a cyclical slowdown that doesn’t seem likely to reverse soon. Given that economic uncertainty has increased in recent weeks due to the U.S. trade war, I think long-term investors should wait for a pullback to enter into a position, or buy more shares, as earnings revisions seem to be biased to the downside ahead which could lead to a lower share price in the near term.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Kimbell Royalty Partners: Reduced Potential Dilution After Partial Redemption Of Preferred Units
data.description:
Kimbell Royalty Partners (NYSE:KRP) redeemed half of its Series A Preferred Units in early May. This slightly reduces its near-term distributable cash flow, but saves it money (or the potential dilution from conversion) in the long-term.

Kimbell's credit facility utilization is near 75% after redeeming those preferred units, which is why it didn't redeem all of its preferred units. It should be able to slowly work down its credit facility debt while keeping its payout ratio at 75%.

Kimbell's Q1 2025 results were solid, as it reached a new high in production due to its Mabee Ranch acquisition. Kimbell declared a distribution of $0.47 per unit in Q1 2025.

Kimbell also reaffirmed its full-year guidance, although I'd expect its quarterly distribution to average around $0.36 per unit during the remainder of the year now. This is due to lower oil prices, with Kimbell having swaps covering close to 20% of its production.

I've reduced my long-term oil price estimate to $70 from $75 now, which is the main contributor to the change in Kimbell's estimated value to $16.75 per unit from $18.50 per unit.

Series A Preferred Unit Redemption

Kimbell redeemed half of its outstanding Series A Cumulative Convertible Preferred Units on May 7, 2025. It redeemed 162,500 Series A Preferred Units (with an issue price of $1,000 per unit) for $182.3 million. This was a redemption price of $1,121.92 per unit.

This results in a slight near-term hit (around $4 million) to Kimbell's distributable cash flow due to the redemption premium and the slightly higher interest rate on its credit facility debt.

At the end of Q1 2025, Kimbell's credit facility debt had a 7.68% interest rate versus the 6% distribution rate on the Series A Preferred Units.

Due to the minimum IRR requirement in the redemption price calculation, the cost to redeem the Series A Preferred Units will keep going up. Thus it makes sense for Kimbell to redeem the units now despite the slight negative near-term impact on distributable cash flow.

Kimbell's credit facility borrowing base (and aggregate commitments) were both increased from $550 million to $625 million with the spring redetermination. After giving effect to the Series A Preferred Unit redemption and the debt paydown from 25% of its Q1 2025 distributable cash flow, Kimbell expects to have around $462 million in credit facility debt.

Kimbell likely would have redeemed more of its Series A Preferred Units if it had more room under its credit facility, but the redemption of half of those units brought its credit facility utilization up to around 74%. Kimbell will now be working down its credit facility debt for a while.

Q1 2025 Results

Kimbell's Q1 2025 production was solid, with 25,501 BOEPD in average production during the quarter. This includes 74 days of production from Kimbell's Mabee Ranch acquisition.

Including the pre-closing production from this deal (with Kimbell receiving the related revenues anyway), Kimbell's Q1 2025 production would have been 25,841 BOEPD. This is slightly above its full-year production guidance midpoint of 25,500 BOEPD.

Kimbell noted it had 90 rigs drilling on its acreage at the end of Q1 2025, down 1 rig from the end of 2024 (including the Mabee Ranch assets). Kimbell's market share remained at 16% of all Continental US land rigs.

The most recent rig count report indicates a roughly 2% decline in Continental US land rigs, so I'd expect around 88 rigs to be drilling on Kimbell's acreage currently.

Updated 2025 Outlook

Kimbell reaffirmed its guidance for around 25,500 BOEPD (33% oil) in 2025 production.

At current strip prices of $63 to $64 WTI oil and $3.90 NYMEX natural gas, Kimbell is now projected to generate $329 million in 2025 revenues after hedges.

Kimbell's hedges are positive value for oil and slightly negative value for natural gas, resulting in an estimated $2 million in positive value for 2025 currently.

Kimbell is now projected to generate $222 million in 2025 distributable cash flow at current strip. This is around $2.06 per unit in distributable cash flow for the full year.

Kimbell has a 75% payout ratio, so it may put $56 million towards debt reduction and $166 million ($1.54 per unit) towards common unit distributions for the year.

Kimbell's Q1 2025 distribution was $0.47 per unit, but its distribution may average a lower amount (such as $0.36 per unit) during the remaining quarters in 2025. This would be due to lower oil prices, as well as a modest impact from the preferred unit redemptions as mentioned above.

Estimated Valuation

Kimbell's Q1 2025 performance was solid, and it reaffirmed its full-year guidance. However, I've tweaked its estimated value downwards from $18.50 per unit down to $16.75 per unit.

This new estimated value is based on long-term (after 2026) $70 WTI oil and $3.75 NYMEX natural gas. The majority of the change is due to my long-term oil price dropping from $75 to $70. This reduces Kimbell's annual distributable free cash flow by around $0.16 per unit.

As well, there is some impact from lower near-term oil prices in 2025 and 2026 based on strip.

Conclusion

Kimbell redeemed half of its $325 million in Series A Preferred Units in early May. This reduces the potential future dilution from the conversion of the preferred units into common units. The move does result in around a $4 million per year near-term reduction in Kimbell's distributable cash flow, but those units get more expensive to redeem as time goes on.

I now expect Kimbell's quarterly distribution to average around $0.36 per unit during the last three quarters of 2025 based on current strip prices. I have also revised its estimated value to $16.75 per unit based on long-term $70 WTI oil and $3.75 NYMEX natural gas.

data.title: SR Bancorp Is Likely To Cool Off This Summer
data.description:
The last couple of years have been a bit of a whirlwind for SR Bancorp (NASDAQ:SRBK). After existing under a number of different names since its beginnings in the late 1800s, the company reorganized in 2023 and completed an IPO valued at just over $90 million. As part of the conversion into a new, publicly traded company, the company also acquired Regal Bancorp, adding three members of Regal's board of directors to its own governing body. When these transactions were complete, SR Bancorp had established a modest footprint in Northern New Jersey, with 14 bank branches and one loan office.

Despite all of this recent activity, the bank operates in virtual anonymity. No Wall Street analysts follow SRBK, and the company has not even been covered by a writer from Seeking Alpha since its IPO. I decided to look into the bank’s operations to see if it could be a diamond in the rough. The stock is up an amazing 43.5% in the last 12 months, but my research tells me that type of price appreciation is unlikely to happen again unless some big changes happen for the company. At best, I believe the stock is worthy of only a hold recommendation for the reasons that I will cover in this article.

Company Overview

With its corporate offices in Bound Brook, NJ, about 25 miles from Newark, SR Bancorp is the holding company for Somerset Regal Bank. SRBK has a market cap of just under $120 million, but somehow the bank felt even smaller when I tried to research its financial information from quarterly and annual reports. The Investor Relations page on the company website has not been updated with news since September 20, 2023. The company lists the name of the CEO, William Taylor, as the only option on its Investor Contacts page, with his phone number and email address.

For the last 12 months, total revenues for the bank were $28 million, but net income was in the red with a loss of about $100,000. The losses can be attributed, in part, to costs from the merger and IPO. At the end of its last quarter, SR Bancorp had just over $835.6 million in deposits and a net loan balance of $780.8 million. Those loans are primarily commercial and residential real estate loans. A snapshot of the company’s loan portfolio from its most recent 10-Q released on May 15 is given below.

If considered by their share of net loans from the bank, residential mortgages compose 53.0% of the portfolio. Commercial loans, which include multifamily loans, constitute most of the balance at 45.5%. The bank states that “nearly all” of its loans are servicing properties within the bank’s primary market area in northern New Jersey.

The stock pays a quarterly dividend of $0.05, giving SRBK a yield of 1.53%. The company has reported $0.34 of earnings in the first three quarters of the current fiscal year, so the dividend appears to be relatively safe at this time.

Analysis of SR Bancorp

Comparisons to financial metrics that go back to the company’s first fiscal year are challenging because of several charges and expenses related to the merger that were non-recurring. The company's fiscal year ends June 30, so for most of my research, I looked at the data from the second half of 2024 (Q1 and Q2) and the first quarter of 2025 (Q3). Unfortunately, all the reported performance ratios for the bank appear to be moving in the wrong direction. Below is a list of data points taken from SRBK’s most recent quarterly reports.

3 Months Ending 9/30/24 (Q1)

3 Months Ending 12/31/24 (Q2)

 3 Months Ending 3/31/25 (Q3)

Return on Average Assets

1.13%

Net Interest Margin

3.21%

2.88%

Efficiency Ratio

81.23%

82.75%

91.41%

GAAP Earnings Per Share

$0.16

$0.12

$0.06

ROA, ROE, and NIM are all getting gradually worse, and the company's earnings per share are following suit. The net interest margin places SRBK slightly below the national average for banks of its size, according to a recent report from the FDIC. The national average for a bank like SR Bancorp with just over $1 billion in total assets is approximately 3.4%.

Margins are a challenge for SR Bancorp because the bank has a high number of interest-bearing accounts, relative to its total deposit base. Out of the $835.6 million in deposits reported at the end of March this year, the company is paying interest on 87.3% of that total, leaving only 12.7% as non-interest-bearing. This is an improvement from Q2 when this number was only 11.5%, but it is still lower than most of its peers.

The percentage of non-interest-bearing deposits at three other banks with similar size and operations are given below. While the comparisons are not exactly apples to apples, each bank selected has a market cap within $10 million of SRBK. Each also has a noticeably higher share of non-interest-bearing accounts.

  • Riverview Bancorp (RVSB) – 25.6%
  • South Atlantic Bancshares (OTCQX:SABK) – 20.8%
  • SB Financial Group (SBFG) – 18.9%

One bright spot I could find for SRBK from the end of its last quarter was a significant increase in non-interest income. Compared to the same time period in the previous fiscal year, this revenue source has increased 41%, or $571,000, to $2.0 million for the nine months ended March 31, 2025. The improvement was made possible by an increase in service charges and bank fees, as well as a $128,000 benefit from the cash surrender value of a bank owned life insurance account. During this same time, non-interest expenses decreased, in part because the bank was finally able to recognize some synergies from its merger with Regal Bancorp.

New Jersey Market Analysis

Because SR Bancorp has all of its offices in the state of New Jersey, the bank is largely at the mercy of the state’s economy. Job growth and economic expansion in the Garden State would be good news for SRBK as well as other local banks, but SR Bancorp is unique in that it is the only publicly traded bank that I could find that operates solely within the state of New Jersey.

The good news for SRBK is that most projections for the state are at least in line with national expectations. GDP in New Jersey is expected to grow by 1.5% in 2025. The state is leading the Northeast in population growth. The number of people living in New Jersey increased by 1.3% in 2024. NJ Labor Commissioner Robert Asaro-Angelo said in January that the state has “more jobs and employers than at any other time in the history of the state.” Nonetheless, unemployment is expected to tick slightly higher this year, peaking around 4.9% compared to 4.6% at the end of 2024.

Because so much of the bank’s loan portfolio is dependent on the housing market and mortgage origination, it is also important to check on expectations for that sector of the state economy. According to Zillow, home sales remain strong in New Jersey, with property values increasing but not as sharply as they have in previous years. The median time on the market for a house is just 20 days. Strong demographics, job growth, and limited supply are all supporting the housing market in the face of elevated interest rates. While none of these facts look like huge green flags for SRBK, there are also no major warning signs at this time.

Risks to Consider

I see three significant risks to consider before investing in SR Bancorp. The first is the chance that we will see a significant slowdown in the overall US economy later this year. Depending on who should be considered the best source, the odds of a recession range from 37%, according to the IMF, to 60%, if JPMorgan is to be believed. If a recession does take place, its length and its severity would impact profit opportunities for all banks in the US, but smaller banks like SR Bancorp could be more severely impacted.

The second risk to consider is the bank’s limited geographical footprint in New Jersey. While the projections mentioned earlier did not seem to show any significant concerns at the current time, there are added risks to investing in a company that is so dependent on one relatively small section of the country. A downturn in employment trends or local political instability could affect SRBK’s bottom line and limit future growth opportunities.

The final risk is the company’s relatively short track record as a publicly traded company. Normally, when I research a stock, I like to see at least three years of financial results, but those do not exist with SRBK. Parsing out the full impact of the company’s addition of Regal Bancorp or comparing like-for-like items in the last annual report are not as simple as they would be if the bank had more years of existence under its belt. The fact that the bank does not seem to update its own Investor Relations website does not help my confidence level.

The Bottom Line

Based on the information that I have been able to gather here, I am holding off on recommending an investment in SRBK for the time being. Investors who have been owners of SR Bancorp over the last year should be thankful for their gains. I think it is safe to assume that the next 12 months may not be as kind to the stock. The risks for the company seem to be at least equal to the potential rewards.

I do think the bank has some value as a potential buyout candidate as the only publicly traded bank to operate exclusively in the state of New Jersey, but when that could happen is anyone's guess at this point. Recent concerns about tariffs and trade wars have made the outlook for most bank mergers and acquisitions exceedingly cloudy. Investors who are looking for another option to invest in a community bank in the Northeast may want to read my recent analysis of Northeast Community Bancorp (NECB), which has increased in value by 11.3% since I rated it as a buy on April 14.
